posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
I’m sure that to many of you in the know this is quite obvious, but why is chardonnay preferentially hit by this? That is, why aren’t the red burgs experiencing premox as well?

Thanks in advance,

Jeremy

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Jeremy C:

I’m sure that to many of you in the know this is quite obvious, but why is chardonnay preferentially hit by this? That is, why aren’t the red burgs experiencing premox as well?

Thanks in advance,

Jeremy

Tannins and phenolics in grape skins are supposedly natural buffets to oxygen.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
c fu:
Jeremy C:

I’m sure that to many of you in the know this is quite obvious, but why is chardonnay preferentially hit by this? That is, why aren’t the red burgs experiencing premox as well?

Thanks in advance,

Jeremy

Tannins and phenolics in grape skins are supposedly natural buffets to oxygen.

Thanks. Yes, I had assumed as much but then I also understood that white burgs used to be able to comfortably age for decades, very similar to reds. Or am I missing something?

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Jeremy C:
c fu:
Jeremy C:

I’m sure that to many of you in the know this is quite obvious, but why is chardonnay preferentially hit by this? That is, why aren’t the red burgs experiencing premox as well?

Thanks in advance,

Jeremy

Tannins and phenolics in grape skins are supposedly natural buffets to oxygen.

Thanks. Yes, I had assumed as much but then I also understood that white burgs used to be able to comfortably age for decades, very similar to reds. Or am I missing something?

The jury is still out on the underlying issue. Most likely it has something to do with the new vinification techniques that were adopted in Burgundy dring the 1990’s. Many believe the main culprit is excessively reductive winemaking, i.e. avoiding oxygen contact as much as possible. This way the wines can be remarkably expresive and fresh upon bottling but react too vigorously with oxygen as the wine ages.

But the phenomenon still remains somewhat of a mystery. Lots of theories revolving around, but nothing conclusive yet. Or there may be several different causes.

If I’ve understood correctly, the red wine vinification techniques haven’t changed much in the past decades, which is why the premox issue is affecting only the whites.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
c fu:
Jeremy C:

I’m sure that to many of you in the know this is quite obvious, but why is chardonnay preferentially hit by this? That is, why aren’t the red burgs experiencing premox as well?

Thanks in advance,

Jeremy

Tannins and phenolics in grape skins are supposedly natural buffets to oxygen.

Basically this. It’s very rare to have an oxidized red of any kind, and when you do, it’s usually a bad cork seal or an extremely old wine.

We mostly observe premox in White Burgundy because of their prominence and the fact that people try to age them, but I think on a proportionate basis, white Bordeaux, Loire chenin and White Rhônes are just as bad.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
Seems strange that there would have been a systematic, near across the board change in vinification of solely the Chardonnay in Burgundy.

Also, there are theories that the closures are contributing. If so, then the reds should also be impacted.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Jeremy C:

Seems strange that there would have been a systematic, near across the board change in vinification of solely the Chardonnay in Burgundy.

Also, there are theories that the closures are contributing. If so, then the reds should also be impacted.

You’re correct - this is one of the additional theories, but it really doesn’t exclude the possibility of other possible theories. It might be possible that some of the producers might have started to produce more reductively, while others have gotten a lousy streak of poor closures. Some might even have a bad case of both at the same time. More unlikely, but not impossible.

Some other theories have been reduction the use of sulfites, longer aging regimes, shorter aging regimes, racking off the lees, excessive bâtonnage, etc. etc.

However, you’re absolutely right in that unless the producers use different corks for different wines, the reds should also be impacted.

The base wines are not the same as before - and it’s not just Chardonnay whites that are affected in this way - the climate is not the same, hence, neither are the grapes.

“Most likely it has something to do with the new vinification techniques that were adopted in Burgundy dring the 1990’s.” It’s simply not credible that virtually every domaine in Burgundy (and around the world) all changed their vinification techniques over a 5-7 year period. There are many, many domaines that changed nothing and still had (have!) problems, as there are very many domains that tried to re-create wine-making exactly as their parents had done - but the wines still oxidised…

posts.name: Chris Bublitz
posts.cooked:
c fu:
Jeremy C:

I’m sure that to many of you in the know this is quite obvious, but why is chardonnay preferentially hit by this? That is, why aren’t the red burgs experiencing premox as well?

Thanks in advance,

Jeremy

We mostly observe premox in White Burgundy because of their prominence and the fact that people try to age them, but I think on a proportionate basis, white Bordeaux, Loire chenin and White Rhônes are just as bad.

Agree, not just white Burgundy - I have had several premoxed white Bordeaux and Loire Chenin blancs.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
I’m aware other white varietals have been observed to oxidize prematurely; my question is specifically about the issue in Burgundy.

If the issue is secondary to the intrinsic properties of the juice derived from the respective grapes, then why would there have been a change 20 years ago? Climate change seems unlikely to impart such a seemingly surgical change over a short time period. Enclosures would seem to impact the other juices. It’s unlikely that a system-wide vilification change occurred at the same relative time for all Chardonnay. And the multiple cause theory seems even less likely to have coalesced at the same time, again, disproportionately with respect to Chardonnay.

And the multiple cause theory seems even less likely to have coalesced at the same time, again, disproportionately with respect to Chardonnay.

Lots and lots of threads deep diving into premox in white burgundy.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
billnanson:

The base wines are not the same as before - and it’s not just Chardonnay whites that are affected in this way - the climate is not the same, hence, neither are the grapes.

“Most likely it has something to do with the new vinification techniques that were adopted in Burgundy dring the 1990’s.” It’s simply not credible that virtually every domaine in Burgundy (and around the world) all changed their vinification techniques over a 5-7 year period. There are many, many domaines that changed nothing and still had (have!) problems, as there are very many domains that tried to re-create wine-making exactly as their parents had done - but the wines still oxidised…

So Bill, are you saying that the climate change reached a critical point in 1995/1996 and thereafter that caused the problem that affected nearly every producer at the same time? And what would account for the bottle to bottle variation within the same case? And why are some producers relatively unaffected such as Coche and Raveneau, while Leflaive who was relatively unaffected until about 2002 or so is now a poster child for prmox? And have the producers who have tried to recreate their parents winemaking gone back to basket presses? Many questions!

It’s unlikely that a system-wide vilification change occurred at the same relative time for all Chardonnay. And the multiple cause theory seems even less likely to have coalesced at the same time, again, disproportionately with respect to Chardonnay.

Lots and lots of threads deep diving into premox in white burgundy.

It’s not like I posted a 22 year old article.

But seriously, I have yet to see any cohesive answers to these questions. They all have seemed like throwing darts on a board.

Yup, that’s it: the vilification change. We’ve all become fussier and less tolerant of these flaws as we’ve become more aware of them. I blame social media, Mark Zuckerberg in particular.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Jeremy C:
c fu:
Jeremy C:

I’m aware other white varietals have been observed to oxidize prematurely; my question is specifically about the issue in Burgundy.

If the issue is secondary to the intrinsic properties of the juice derived from the respective grapes, then why would there have been a change 20 years ago? Climate change seems unlikely to impart such a seemingly surgical change over a short time period. Enclosures would seem to impact the other juices. It’s unlikely that a system-wide vilification change occurred at the same relative time for all Chardonnay. And the multiple cause theory seems even less likely to have coalesced at the same time, again, disproportionately with respect to Chardonnay.

Lots and lots of threads deep diving into premox in white burgundy.

It’s not like I posted a 22 year old article.

But seriously, I have yet to see any cohesive answers to these questions. They all have seemed like throwing darts on a board.

And that’s because there isn’t really one. Just speculation and opinions.

Three big things play into the persistence of the problem:

(1) it’s hard to experiment with solutions, since you won’t know if they succeed unless and until you wait 5-10 years and open a lot of bottles;

(2) most producers are small and also deny or minimize the existence of the problem, so who has an incentive to spend a lot of effort and money to crack the code?

(3) sales and pricing remain very strong, so again, how much motivation do they have to study and to change things? Plus, it’s possible the “solution” may be something (harder pressing? Earlier picking?) that makes their products less marketable.

I think the simple way to look at it is this: the wines in the last 15-20 years seem designed to drink perfectly well when they are young (my personal opinion is that this is the reason or part of the reason they don’t resist oxidation well now). They have ripe fruits and acids that are not overbearing, even in Chablis. So just drink them young - say within 5-6 years I’d the vintage. If you really want ones for aging, read Don Cornwell’s wiki and see which producers have the best track record, and also just do it knowing you’re taking a risk.

That’s all we can really do as consumers.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
The other thing is — damn, it sure would be nice if they were willing to use screw caps. Corks may not be the cause of the problem, but even if they aren’t, screw caps could still be the solution. Long term tests of screw caps on white wines show very convincingly that they resist oxidation.

But between lack of consumer acceptance of screw caps for premium wines, and the producers really not admitting to the problem or needing to try to solve it, it’s not going to happen.

Domaine Laroche used screwcaps for some grand cru Chablis. I opened a 2001 Les Clos a year or two ago and it was in perfect condition. They recently switched back to cork.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
Some have suggested that screw cap enclosure leads toward a too reductive effect. Although that opinion might be based on older screw caps whereas now they (may) have so improved on fine tuning oxygen transmission that that issue is no longer such a concern. Besides, the risk of TCA is arguably greater anyway and screw cap would perhaps kill both of those birds.

But unless there was a systematic Burgundy-specific change in corks in the mid-1990s this doesn’t seem to really answer the question. Additionally, though I understand that reds have the additional tannic protection, one should assume some degree of increased red premox during this period such that at this point some of the 1995 and 1996 red bugs would have already demonstrated a greater than historical rate of oxidation.

Outside of the red vs white query (which is easily answered and has been already), the flaw in the question is assuming that Chardonnay is “preferentially hit” - what evidence is there for that?

I would say I have had an equal premox rate with white bordeaux to white burgundy. I’d argue that the reason there appears to be more of it in white burgundy than elsewhere is that there’s a lot more money being thrown down for larger volumes of wine, and as such the problems are more likely to surface publicly. Further to that, there is far more wealth of knowledge regarding how white burgundy should age and therefore the expectation is there.

I bet no end of non-white burg gets shoved down the drain without being recognized for premox.

I’ve had premoxed white bordeaux on many, many occasions in basically every vintage ranging from 1991 (Pavillion Blanc - I don’t think this should have been oxidised which is why I consider it premox, as it was multiple bottles and thus didn’t seem to be a closure issue) to 2010. 1994 is a famous one for DDC as every single bottle I’ve read about being consumed and consumed myself (and that’s a lot now) has been a dud.

Onto the actual topic of why premox is even something we have to cope with, this is easily one of the most compelling discussions I’ve read on it in quite a while: Black Chardonnay Comes From Re-Discovered Winemaking Magic | SevenFifty Daily

link_counts.title: Black Chardonnay Comes From Re-Discovered Winemaking Magic | SevenFifty Daily

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
julianseersmartin:

Onto the actual topic of why premox is even something we have to cope with, this is easily one of the most compelling discussions I’ve read on it in quite a while: Black Chardonnay Comes From Re-Discovered Winemaking Magic | SevenFifty Daily

Hi, Julian. Thanks for your response.

You bring up many separate issues that I’d like to address:

To your first point, whereas it may be that the “red vs. white query” is easily answered, I’ve yet to see one with which I’m satisfied. Perhaps you could proffer one?

To your second point, respectfully I disagree. I made no such assumption. In fact, I definitely do not believe that premox only afflicts chardonnay in Burgundy. I’m merely wanting to discuss the issue of premox with white Burgundy as there is where the greatest noise is.

Your other comments are certainly appreciated and I’ll definitely peruse the discussion you linked.

Cheers,

Jeremy

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Jeremy C:

But unless there was a systematic Burgundy-specific change in corks in the mid-1990s this doesn’t seem to really answer the question. Additionally, though I understand that reds have the additional tannic protection, one should assume some degree of increased red premox during this period such that at this point some of the 1995 and 1996 red bugs would have already demonstrated a greater than historical rate of oxidation.

I don’t think is a promising avenue of inquiry. Red wine premox is so incredibly rare. I can only think of one bottle I’ve ever had - a sadly, 375 of 1995 Musar, which is such a great wine

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I think about five hundred pages have been devoted to this issue, but all of us are looking at it from the outside. I noticed two things in the '90s:
use of lower amount of SO2
everybody bought a new press

At the same time, the world got hotter and one study found by Don Cornwell illustrated that must weights in Burgundy reached a record high in 2005. So at a time of higher must weights folks were using less SO2
when they should have been using more.

The issue of the new presses confused me, until I read an article about glutathione. Glutathione is a powerful anti oxidant found in lees. What do the new presses do?? Deliver juice with less solids.

Now somewhere on this site one can find the results of Don’s annual ‘how much white Burgundy can I pour?’ tastings and it would seem the Burgundians are starting to figure things out.

Another question might be, Why don’t we see this problem in California. It seems to me that the wines that aged well in the 80s and 90s are still doing so.

A lot of people in Burgundy have switched to the Diam cork. The idea here is that the culprit was bad corks. This should help but I don t think it is the solution.

Others have discussed the issue of reductive winemaking. This is a good point. If you want to sell your wine in a tony restaurant it helps that the wine does not smell like rotten eggs. So the wine is great two years after bottling but maybe it does not age as well.

One winemaker said to me, Most of my wine is drunk two years after bottling, but if it does not age ten years somebody gets mad.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

I think about five hundred pages have been devoted to this issue, but all of us are looking at it from the outside. I noticed two things in the '90s:
use of lower amount of SO2
everybody bought a new press

At the same time, the world got hotter and one study found by Don Cornwell illustrated that must weights in Burgundy reached a record high in 2005. So at a time of higher must weights folks were using less SO2
when they should have been using more.

The issue of the new presses confused me, until I read an article about glutathione. Glutathione is a powerful anti oxidant found in lees. What do the new presses do?? Deliver juice with less solids.

Now somewhere on this site one can find the results of Don’s annual ‘how much white Burgundy can I pour?’ tastings and it would seem the Burgundians are starting to figure things out.

Another question might be, Why don’t we see this problem in California. It seems to me that the wines that aged well in the 80s and 90s are still doing so.

A lot of people in Burgundy have switched to the Diam cork. The idea here is that the culprit was bad corks. This should help but I don t think it is the solution.

Others have discussed the issue of reductive winemaking. This is a good point. If you want to sell your wine in a tony restaurant it helps that the wine does not smell like rotten eggs. So the wine is great two years after bottling but maybe it does not age as well.

One winemaker said to me, Most of my wine is drunk two years after bottling, but if it does not age ten years somebody gets mad.

Bingo on all this. There was the substitution of silicone for paraffin as the coating on many corks without telling winemakers. That may have accelerated the oxidation process.

Is any domaine in Burgundy that anyone knows about implementing or experimenting with a return to the older presses or that style of press in combination with restoration of traditional sulphur treatments / traditional reductive winemaking? Are those domaines that use corkl insisting on paraffin coatings? Or does no one really care if they can sell their wines without doing anything?

It seems to me the natural potential fix is, let’s make it how we used to, for those committed to making vin de Garde and not wines to be drunk in the first 2-5 years. Then adjustments for ripeness / climate change (if any) can follow from that base. It is very strange to me that many domaines eagerly replaced their presses in the mid-90s, tweak their winemaking for stylistic reasons, and then throw up their hands and shrug on premox. Anyway preaching to the choir… I don’t really buy the stuff much anymore. Or Clos St Hune.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Yes, some people have gone back to using their old presses, or so I have heard. Nobody talks about this in public so without visiting every domaine it is hard to know what is going on. Of course, the new presses can be set in any different ways. They don’t really have to go back to the old presses.

If somebody using Diam gets premoxed, there goes the closure issue as a cause.

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

I think about five hundred pages have been devoted to this issue, but all of us are looking at it from the outside. I noticed two things in the '90s:
use of lower amount of SO2
everybody bought a new press

At the same time, the world got hotter and one study found by Don Cornwell illustrated that must weights in Burgundy reached a record high in 2005. So at a time of higher must weights folks were using less SO2
when they should have been using more.

The issue of the new presses confused me, until I read an article about glutathione. Glutathione is a powerful anti oxidant found in lees. What do the new presses do?? Deliver juice with less solids.

I agree Glutathione is a significant issue for Chardonnay/otherWhites, and is something I’ve been interested in for a while. Glutathione’s path from grape to wine is a bit tortured: it’s formed in the leaves and transported to the grapes (into the juice)…it’s formed at/after veraison mainly I believe. During fermentation, yeast consumes most/all of the juice’s Glutathione (hence the level of G at the end of Ferm is v low). As the yeast/lees breakdown in barrel, the G is re-released back into the wine.

So pressing and juice solids doesn’t have anything (directly) to do with G levels in the juice/wine. Course, juice solids = phenolics, and phenolics + G makes a great anti-oxidant team (low pH is an important member of the team as well).

Depending on the growing conditions, the G levels in just picked grapes can vary widely (less than 10 mg/l to 90+mg/l, which is a pretty wide range). I wonder if something changed in the late 90s to lower G levels…climate related maybe? Or, or in addition, perhaps some folks started racking off the lees too early, before the lees had fully released their G back into the wine.

Also, not related to G, but it’s my impression that Casein fining was relatively common for white burgs in the early 90s & before, and then fell out of favor. Once consequence of Casein fining is it significantly lowers the Copper & Iron levels in wine. Copper & Iron are essential for oxidation to occur…no copper/iron = no oxidation!

Mel Knox:

Now somewhere on this site one can find the results of Don’s annual ‘how much white Burgundy can I pour?’ tastings and it would seem the Burgundians are starting to figure things out.

Another question might be, Why don’t we see this problem in California. It seems to me that the wines that aged well in the 80s and 90s are still doing so.

A lot of people in Burgundy have switched to the Diam cork. The idea here is that the culprit was bad corks. This should help but I don t think it is the solution.

Others have discussed the issue of reductive winemaking. This is a good point. If you want to sell your wine in a tony restaurant it helps that the wine does not smell like rotten eggs. So the wine is great two years after bottling but maybe it does not age as well.

One winemaker said to me, Most of my wine is drunk two years after bottling, but if it does not age ten years somebody gets mad.

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

(Lines deleted)
A lot of people in Burgundy have switched to the Diam cork. The idea here is that the culprit was bad corks. This should help but I don t think it is the solution.

(More lines deleted)



Mel Knox:

If somebody using Diam gets premoxed, there goes the closure issue as a cause.

Some time ago (10+ years ago?), the BIVB (a Burgundy industry group) did a study of premox in the hopes of finding the solution. The study assumed, or looked for, a single cause of premox. They didn’t find one (I’d love to look at the results of the study!). Assuming their research was well done, then premox is caused by a confluence of things (hence Diam or other closures will/might help but won’t solve the problem on their own).

Also, I’ve said this several times before, but 2007/2008 Fevres are an excellent laboratory for Diam: 07/08 Fevre 1er crus are under Diam5 & 07/08 Grand Crus are under cork. The Grand Crus started premoxing a few years ago. So far the 1er crus are holding up well (based on cellertracker, burg wiki et al).

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Eric,

Maybe we should blame wine schools for the problem. Something I have noticed with some but not all French winemakers when they move here: they rack off the lees very quickly.

As you say, there are many issues here. Using less SO2 as your pH is rising isn’t going to help.

Re corks: I guess what has always bothered me about this issue is that one is saying that Burgundy got bad corks and the rest of the world got the good ones.

I have often said that we should look at people whose whites are ageing well. Two in California are Au Bon Climat and Mt Eden and both winemakers are big on lees.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Red wines’ components benefit more from oxygen and are sturdier, IMO.

I had 4 bottles of WB this week that were OTH…and one of them that was also corked. (And one good CCharl).

RE: corks…I believe that the bad bottles ARE the extent of the problem. And, that the seals are the issue. Some corks are randomly too rigid to make good seals at the bottle necks. Whether the cork/neck combo in the WB bottle is particularly susceptible to bad seals with the randomly rigid silicone-coated corks…has always intrigued me. Clearly, not all white wine bottles are the same as those for WB.

posts.name: Jeff Stettner
posts.cooked: I had an interesting experience tonight. Opened two Girardin whites (375’s), an 05 Puligny VV and an 05 Corton Charlemagne. The CC had a cork and was fully oxidative, dark in color and extremely aged on the palate. There was clearly some good stuff in there at one point but it was very disappointing. The Puligny, on the other hand, was a crew cap. And totally delicious. Riper in style (05 Vintage) but completely lively with some sense of its 12-years but none of the tired qualities of the Grand Cru. The beat goes on.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked: Which Grand Crus are now using a non-cork enclosure? Fevre is, since at least 2010.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked: It does make sense that if you replace a porous seal with an airtight one, there won’t be much more oxidation.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

It does make sense that if you replace a porous seal with an airtight one, there won’t be much more oxidation.

It’s my understanding that screw caps are engineered to allow for a specified amount of air transfer.

posts.name: William Woodruff
posts.cooked: One of the producers I represent switched to Ardeal Seal in 2003. Nary a premox problem.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Jeremy - I’ll try not to repeat what others have said.

  1. This doesn’t affect all producers equally, but there were widespread changes in winemaking in Burgundy, including picking riper fruit with lower acids (whether because of warm vintages or by choice) and reduced use of sulfur. I don’t understand the changes in lees contact, but that’s often cited as well, as others have said. Also, as I understand it, winemakers in Burgundy were less inclined to let their musts oxidize, which can actually make a wine less vulnerable to oxidation later by removing some components.

  2. The problem isn’t limited to Burgundy. In the Loire, Baumard and Huet had premox problems in the early 2000s. Gulfi, a Sicilian producer of very interesting whites with low sulfur levels, had terrible premox issues in the early 2000s. And in the past, most California chardonnays oxidized after five or seven years (Mel’s counter-examples and a few others like Chalone notwithstanding). That seems to have changed in the last decade, from what I’ve experienced, presumably due to changes in winemaking.

  3. There is huge variation for individual wines, even in the same case. Some are sound and others are shot. Variations in the corks seem to be the only possible explanation for that. But I don’t think anyone knows whether changes in corks (e.g., silicon) contributed to premox or if the inherent variations in cork simply exposed wines that were more more vulnerable to oxidation when they went in the bottle.

Steve Tanzer did a very interesting video interview with Dave Ramey of Ramey Wine Cellars on this topic that you should definitely watch. Ramey is very knowledgeable about Burgundy and he did research on oxidation in chardonnays several decades back at Simi.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
John,
My point about California is that the whites that aged well in the 80s still age well and the ones that go south still go south.

Three wineries that make Chardonnays that age well: Ramey, ABC and Mt eden…all bottled with relatively high levels of SO2 and the wines are aged in barrel with a goodly amount of lees.

My 2005 Huets went south faster than I could open bottles.

Sometimes screw caps were so air tight the wines became reductive. But I admit I don t follow the wonderful world of screw caps.
On the other hand, Uvaggio has 2009 Lodi Vermentino sealed with Diam and the wine is still drinking fine.

Dave Ramey and Zelma Long pioneered must oxygenation here. When I took a winemaker from Meursault to visit Dave around 1985, he explained what he was doing and the French guy said, I’ve always wondered why the must turned brown and then clear again.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
In my brown-bag group, for many years we pretty much never had a Cal chardonnay with more than five or seven years that wasn’t premoxed. (People weren’t bringing ABC or Ramey or Chalone.) In more recent years, I’ve had quite a few that have held up better than that, so the conventional techniques have evolved.

Mel Knox:

My 2005 Huets went south faster than I could open bottles.

And I had several bottles of the '02 petillant that had premoxed. I think Berserker Jay Miller had the same experience. Lots of bottle variation on that one.

Mel Knox:

Dave Ramey and Zelma Long pioneered must oxygenation here. When I took a winemaker from Meursault to visit Dave around 1985, he explained what he was doing and the French guy said, I’ve always wondered why the must turned brown and then clear again.

I dimly recall hearing that this was something that some or many Burgundy producers began to discourage in recent decades.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Karl K:

I was going to say what Eric and John pointed out about higher pH as a driving cause, but I don’t see how that accounts for the wide variability.

IMO,only the corks (or manufacturing variances in the bottle necks on the rigid, silicone corks can account for that. Obviously, the “premox” is the bad bottles,not the good wines that result. So…the cork did something.IMHO,it removed the vital SO2 treatment, even more vital with more vulnerable wines…but where the SO2 worked…the way it was supposed to the wines were hearty enough (hearty burgundy?)…to show just fine.

One producer told me early on…that he tested the bad and good bottles in one case of hiswine…a few years after bottling…that was still at the domaine. The bad bottles had no SO2 left…the good bottles, as expected levels given the age…of the bottles…almost that of bottling. I’ve never heard anything else remotely convinincing in the 13 years since he told me of his experience. And, since there are good bottles…I attribute the premox ie, the bad bottles to poor seals due to the rigidity of the corks, which are randomly rigid. That, to me, means that they make random seals at the crucial points of the bottle neck for good seal. (But, boy do they slide out beautifully…all 4 bottles this week of 99’s came out “like buttah”.

The cork industry, IMO, is to blame,not the winemakers or the climate for “the premox”. The wines were/are good enough to last fine if the SO2 added at bottling remains sufficiently intact.

You can check out some of the corks yourself…from good and bad bottles…there is no uniformity of rigidity…both among the corks…and on any given cork, in many cases. Squeeze 'em at various spots on them…some are totally rigid…other…have soft spots…like untreated or parrafin-treated corks. It all makes sense to me given how corks were coated…and supplied…without any real info or notices to the winemakers.

I’m not sure that even if they wanted them, the winemakers had access to softer corks.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Stuart - You have made this argument over and over for years that it’s all the corks. But I don’t think anyone else thinks that’s the only factor, and I don’t think many people think it’s a major factor. Nor do I recall anyone ever buying into your idea that faulty closures somehow reduce sulfur.

There were always bad corks and good. So why all the problems since the mid-90s? Plainly something is quite different about the wine that has gone in the bottle in the last several decades. And we know that much white Burgundy is being made today with lower acids, less sulfur, less lees contact and probably less oxidation of the must than in the past. I don’t know why you deny those factors.

posts.name: William Woodruff
posts.cooked: Stuart, I am in complete agreement. The variance in porousness of closures (ie corks) must be the underlying cause. Therefore, occams razor applies to premox as the most reasonable explanation.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

Stuart - You have made this argument over and over for years that it’s all the corks. But I don’t think anyone else thinks that’s the only factor, and I don’t think many people think it’s a major factor.

Not so sure about the latter half of that statement. There is another thread on the board looking a lot 2009 white burgs and everything closed with diam is sound.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
William Woodruff:

Stuart, I am in complete agreement. The variance in porousness of closures (ie corks) must be the underlying cause. Therefore, occams razor applies to premox as the most reasonable explanation.

Huh?

I agree that cork variations seem to be the only possible explanation for the inconsistency/variability even within the same batch of wine. But corks have always been highly variable, yet in the past white Burgundy could age.

For some reason, the wine that has gone into the bottle since the mid-90s has become more vulnerable to the minute amounts of oxygen that get through corks. If it were just a change in cork – silicon instead of wax, or whatever – you’d expect the incidence of premox would be the same for all producers, but it isn’t. So, even if there were some changes in cork, that alone can’t explain the evidence.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
A.Gillette:
John Morris:

Stuart - You have made this argument over and over for years that it’s all the corks. But I don’t think anyone else thinks that’s the only factor, and I don’t think many people think it’s a major factor.

Not so sure about the latter half of that statement. There is another thread on the board looking a lot 2009 white burgs and everything closed with diam is sound.

If you use a closure that really doesn’t allow air/oxygen to pass, that might solve the problem. But for the reasons I’ve given, it doesn’t explain what changed 20 years ago, or why some producers have a good track record on premox using natural cork.

Not so sure about the latter half of that statement. There is another thread on the board looking a lot 2009 white burgs and everything closed with diam is sound.

If you use a closure that really doesn’t allow air/oxygen to pass, that might solve the problem. But for the reasons I’ve given, it doesn’t explain what changed 20 years ago, or why some producers have a good track record on premox using natural cork.

Corks may not be the sole explanation, but it seems almost unimaginable that they aren’t a significant contributing factor. And the diam corks do allow oxygen to pass. They just control permeability in a predetermined rate.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
A.Gillette:

Corks may not be the sole explanation, but it seems almost unimaginable that they aren’t a significant contributing factor.

Do you mean that something changed with corks that leads to premox? Or just that the fact that some corks have always allowed a fair deal of air penetration is a factor in leading to so many wines now oxydizing? Those are quite different assumptions.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
John Morris:
A.Gillette:

Corks may not be the sole explanation, but it seems almost unimaginable that they aren’t a significant contributing factor.

Do you mean that something changed with corks that leads to premox? Or just that the fact that some corks have always allowed a fair deal of air penetration is a factor in leading to so many wines now oxydizing? Those are quite different assumptions.

I haven’t heard any proof that corks got worse, although I suppose it’s possible. But I dont think that corks needed to get worse in order for them to be a cause of premox. Corks have always been, and still are, imperfect. I think that various winemaking changes in the 90s resulted in wines that could no longer tolerate cork imperfections that previously weren’t such a big deal. In other words, cork differences that prior to ‘96 resulted in variations of maturity and inconsistent bottles of the same wine bottles suddenly resulted in premature oxidation. I think it’s incredibly telling that so many white burgundy producers are switching to synthetic corks for their whites (and in many cases not the reds). And it seems to be working pretty well based on what I’ve seen.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Then we agree completely!

Now what do we do?

posts.name: Alexander Gillette

Hoard German reisling while it remains affordable.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
One way of looking at this whole issue is to look at winemaking as a system of choices leading to a desired end. When you change one link in the process you have to look at what else is being impacted.

When the wine is somewhat reductive and full of SO2, you probably don t have to worry about bottling or corks. But when you are trying to make fruity wine that won t upset some hedge fund operator in Manhattan and you lower the levels of SO2 in the wine then you will probably need a state of the art bottling line that allows for no oxygen pickup and perfect corks. Throw global warming into the picture and you have a scenario that calls for more SO2, not less.

I never quite understood the role of new presses until I read the Dubourdieu/Lavigne article about glutathione but now I see how the newer presses could reduce the amount of glutathione and thus make pH and SO2 carry the load.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Sometimes screw caps were so air tight the wines became reductive. But I admit I don t follow the wonderful world of screw caps.
On the other hand, Uvaggio has 2009 Lodi Vermentino sealed with Diam and the wine is still drinking fine.

This was a problem with earlier generations of screwcap (based on what is now believed to be the mistaken idea that the best corks provided a perfect seal) but they now allow for variable oxygen input.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
Thanks everyone for your valued input.

As I see it, there really are two issues. One, what causes premature oxidation? And two, what changed a little over twenty years ago which seemingly increased the risk of premox?

Whereas this topic has certainly been addressed many times previously, it certainly doesn’t seem like there is any real consensus on either of these two concerns. As far as the cork issue is concerned, I don’t think that the (likely) fact that improvements in enclosures (e.g., DIAM) having made a decrease in the risk of premox proves that traditional corks were previously the cause. Traditional corks fail all the time, and did so before this issue erupted and will continue to do so. It’s not likely (to me, at least) that there was some significant change in the corks that Burgundy was getting which seemingly suddenly caused premox to rear its ugly head. But if it were the case that the enclosures were the main culprit (e.g., with respect to silicon use), then I’m not as yet convinced that the reds would remain completely unaffected. Yes, they wouldn’t oxidize as early, but it just seems likely that they might simply oxidize faster than would have been expected. So, instead of premox erupting in a five year old white, one might see it in a ten year old red.

With respect to winemaking changes around that time, I suppose this is the most likely reason for the perceived change, though it still seems tough that so many (though of course not all) producers would have made such similar changes at essentially the same time.

But I am also unaware as to what exactly the premox rate was before 1995 (or thereabout). I don’t have the personal experience to shed any light on this, but I wonder if some of the memories of the consistency of white Burgundy are a bit too rosy.

posts.name: John Bashford
posts.cooked:
I’ve been a long term enthusiast for White Burgundy and remain dismayed by the lack of progress with this problem. Even more concerning is the continued lack of acknowledgement from some growers that this is an issue. More encouraging are the efforts of makers such as Bouchard with Diam. However as an Australian we sit on sidelines knowing that screw cap closures have resulted in an extraordinary consistency with our favourite local white wines of all types eg the great Leeuwin Estate Art Series Chardonnay. This long running ‘experiment’ ( 20 years plus ) is game, set and match as far as Australian and NZ wine enthusiasts are concerned yet round and round the arguments go in France ! Given the option I would certainly have every bottle of Grand Cru White Burgundy that I purchase sealed with a Stelvin closure. So simple, so reliable and so clearly proven.
Last week we had two bottles of 2005 Jadot PM Clos de la Garenne. The first was moderately oxidised and not worth drinking. The second from the same case was pristine and thoroughly delicious. No matter what has changed with the wine-making, and I’m sure that it’s critically important to this story, only a closure issue will explain the random nature of these failures.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Jeremy C:

With respect to winemaking changes around that time, I suppose this is the most likely reason for the perceived change, though it still seems tough that so many (though of course not all) producers would have made such similar changes at essentially the same time.

But I am also unaware as to what exactly the premox rate was before 1995 (or thereabout). I don’t have the personal experience to shed any light on this, but I wonder if some of the memories of the consistency of white Burgundy are a bit too rosy.

It’s not like the problems commenced on the same year with every producer.

But it really is the case that changes in winemaking occur in waves/fads. In Barolo, a large number of people changed the way they made the wines pretty radically in a very short period of time in the late 80s and early 90s (very short fermentation times in roto-fermenters; malolactic fermentation and aging in new oak, etc.). Similarly, in Bordeaux, reverse osmosis for concentration became extremely common in the 1990s. In California in the same period, there was a pretty dramatic shift toward picking at much higher Brix – enough so that average alcohols in Napa wines rose 1.5% over a dozen years or so. And reverse osmosis or cones came into vogue to reduce the resulting alcohol, along with water additions.

I gather that in Burgundy a lot of producers bought gentler presses starting in the '90s (reducing skin extraction that may help preserve the wine), and there was a trend toward picking later for richer. Both were done to produce more approachable wines. On top of that, people began reducing their sulfur additions.

People talk to their neighbors and friends, watch what their rivals do, and listen to the same sales people. So there’s a lot of group think. It doesn’t take long for a fad to sweep a region.

posts.name: William Woodruff
posts.cooked:
If one we’re to bottle 300 bottles of wine, from a single barrel (or tank) and 15% of said bottles are premixed and the others are fine, the causes must be one of the following;

  1. Glass variance
  2. Closure variance

We have noted incidents of “three pristine bottles, nine shot” out of case." If wine making were the cause, the entire barrel (or tank) should be premoxed. These “three pristine bottles” cannot originate from an entire premoxed barrel (or tank).

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I used to sell bottles for a company that made the same bottle every day. The bore entry was always perfect. Many people use special bottles where this may not be the case.

One more point about glutathione: Lavigne and Dubourdieu point out that Burgundians used to employ herbicide to kill cover crop but now that everyone has gone bio, they let the cover crop flourish. Under stress conditions such as drought, the vine and the cover crop compete for nutrients that help create glutathione.


My usual reaction to two bad bottles in a carton: the canary in the mine. Time to run to safety.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

I have often said that we should look at people whose whites are ageing well. Two in California are Au Bon Climat and Mt Eden and both winemakers are big on lees.

Great point and perfect examples Mel.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

Stuart - You have made this argument over and over for years that it’s all the corks. But I don’t think anyone else thinks that’s the only factor, and I don’t think many people think it’s a major factor. Nor do I recall anyone ever buying into your idea that faulty closures somehow reduce sulfur.

There were always bad corks and good. So why all the problems since the mid-90s? Plainly something is quite different about the wine that has gone in the bottle in the last several decades. And we know that much white Burgundy is being made today with lower acids, less sulfur, less lees contact and probably less oxidation of the must than in the past. I don’t know why you deny those factors.

John :

I have not made “this” argument for even a year.Last May I made contact with a cork industry official…and obtained a thorough description of the silicone-coating process for corks (haphazzard to say the least, as to any given cork; a giant washing machine splashing the stuff around, essentially). Then , before the guy stopped talking to me, I did some online research and saw videos of the process, etc. Then I became convinced “it’s all the closures”, ie. the seals, the corks’ seal with the bottle neck.

Before that I had been shown by two respected Burg producers the irregular rigidity of those corks and the loss of SO2 in the bad bottles…in 2007. And, found out that there was no notification re: change from parrafin to silicone coationgs…which made the corks rigid…though not uniformly within any batch…or even on any particular cork. The makers were in the dark.

Using the “logic” expressed here, particularly, well articulated by Mr. Woodruff, above, I decided then to focus my own 'inquiry" to the “good” bottles, which were plentiful and present within most given cases/batches of bottled wines.If they were fine, I reasoned,the wines, themselves, were sufficiently durable to last and prosper. The “premox” was only the bad bottles. No matter what the changes were…method or climatic… the wines themselves weren’t the problem…or a sufficient 'problem" to be the variable that made the premox variable. This is only “logical”.

“Bad corks” were always around, but that is highly misleading and irrelevant to this inquiry. Bad corks hardly manifested their charms by creating poor seals. Those problems were distinct, usually through polluting the wine , like a moldy teabag would your morning tea.

So, I ask you…and others: how do you account for the random intact/fine bottles if you think it’s the wine?? What could the variable be IN THE WINE that causes some to go bad and leaves some pristine?

Oh, and add the factor that the silicone coatings,to help the cork slide out better than the older ones coated with paraffin , were increasingly applied to wine corks in Portugal, without any notice, warning…or control on the part of the winemakers. And, it can’t be some fuzzy coincidence.

Re: why WB particularly? If that’s true…there are two parts to the seal for any given bottle:the cork and the neck itself.I know nothing about WB bottle necks, but…doubt they are that variably manufactured to be a problem. And,as you pointed out, they worked fine before the corks were coated differently…to create good seals.

So…how do you explain the good bottles within any random case/group if the wine is a problem? Let’s focus on that…and that we know there was not an en masse change in winemaking at that juncture…and that SO2 had worked just fine before “global warming” and seems to work just fine for many many bottles after “Burgundian warming”?

I also think that that’s why the wine industry has never figured out what they’ve done wrong.And,the cork industry…pretends to deny its role…and, in fact, “told” me that the problem in WB had been fixed…ha ha…


So…let’s move away from our fuzzy logic here already. Focus on the good bottles and tell me how they still exisit…

posts.name: Robert Love
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

So, I ask you…and others: how do you account for the random intact/fine bottles if you think it’s the wine?? What could the variable be IN THE WINE that causes some to go bad and leaves some pristine?

One hypothesis is that premox is caused by a combination of corks and some other change e.g. changes in bâtonnage or pressing. That change lowers the wine’s resistance to oxidation; the variability of corks results in that oxidation applied unevenly.

As John has pointed out, if your theory of the case is that corks alone are the problem, one mystery goes unsolved: Why do some producers have horrible premox rates while others excellent track records?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

“Bad corks” were always around, but that is highly misleading and irrelevant to this inquiry. Bad corks hardly manifested their charms by creating poor seals. Those problems were distinct, usually through polluting the wine , like a moldy teabag would your morning tea.

You’re right. Corkiness/TCA, which has nothing to do with premox.

But there certainly are “bad corks” that don’t provide an ideal seal. Cork is highly variable in the amount of air/oxygen that can penetrate it, and that’s always been true.

navoidable natural flaws, channels, and cracks in the bark make the cork itself highly inconsistent. In a 2005 closure study, 45% of corks showed gas leakage during pressure testing both from the sides of the cork as well as through the cork body itself.
(–> Wikipedia> )


Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

So, I ask you…and others: how do you account for the random intact/fine bottles if you think it’s the wine?? What could the variable be IN THE WINE that causes some to go bad and leaves some pristine?

…

So…how do you explain the good bottles within any random case/group if the wine is a problem? Let’s focus on that…and that we know there was not an en masse change in winemaking at that juncture…and that SO2 had worked just fine before “global warming” and seems to work just fine for many many bottles after “Burgundian warming”?

How do I explain it? Easy – (a) changes in winemaking that have made the wine more vulnerable to oxygen plus (b) the variations in permeabilily of natural corks that has always been there.

How can you assert that “we know there was not an en masse change in winemaking at that juncture” when we know that since the 1990s Burgundy producers have been picking later (lower acid), switching to new presses that extract less from the skins, reducing lees contact and reducing the amount of sulfur they use? You just ignore all of that.

I ask you: If it were just a change in corks, why has white Burgundy been so plagued? Why isn’t premox more widespread in other regions?

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
As John states, the imperfections of cork enclosure may simply have been “masked” by better (from an age-ability perspective) winemaking practices prior to the premox period. It could simply have been a matter of threshold. And once that threshold was met, the variability of the corks disclose the “flaws.” More successful enclosures could essentially be moving the threshold higher.

Do we know that the producers of the wines most susceptible to premox during this period were also the ones who made significant changes to their respective winemaking (new presses, later harvest, etc.) and that those with more robust wines did not make those changes?

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
It used to be said that the wine breathed through the cork and this helped the wine age…a very very slow micro oxidation process.

Now, are we saying that this is a bad thing and that the perfect stopper seals the wine so it remains as it was at bottling?? I suppose one could say this was true if the wine has been brought to perfection at bottling, but this would be a new idea for ultra premium wine. Or are we saying that wine develops anaerobically??

Let’s ask ourselves the question, What helps white wine age and develop?
1/SO2
2/acidity…low pH
3/glutathione
4/a good seal…reminds me of the polar bear joke

If we eliminate one of these things, the others have to get stronger. If we eliminate two of them, the other two have to pump iron.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

It used to be said that the wine breathed through the cork and this helped the wine age…a very very slow micro oxidation process.

I suppose one could say this was true if the wine has been brought to perfection at bottling, but this would be a new idea for ultra premium wine. Or are we saying that wine develops anaerobically??

I’m not clear on that. I thought there were some people who think that the air in the headspace under the cork suffices. And some research has suggested that the very best (if that’s the word) corks allow pretty much now air penetration, which would support that view.

Mel Knox:

Let’s ask ourselves the question, What helps white wine age and develop?
1/SO2

3/glutathione
4/a good seal…reminds me of the polar bear joke

If we eliminate one of these things, the others have to get stronger. If we eliminate two of them, the other two have to pump iron.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:
John Morris:

I ask you: If it were just a change in corks, why has white Burgundy been so plagued? Why isn’t premox more widespread in other regions?

It is a big problem in many regions. We had a massive problem in Australia right through the 90’s. On mass, the industry changed to screwcap, led by the Clare Valley Riesling producers around 2001 and 2002. No problems now.

Jeremy,

The problem in Australia was due to very high incidents of TCA, correct? I don’t remember it being due to random oxidation, but that has always been a problem with natural cork - and will remain so.

I remember reading the original AWRI study on closures and was blown away by the variability in trans-ox on corks, including the ‘best’ ones. It does appear that the ‘best’ corks - the ones that led to the most preferred wines - did allow in some oxygen, but very little.

Those calling for a ‘perfect’ closure where no oxygen is allowed in - this will lead to reduction, maybe sooner, maybe later. Of course, I’ll take a reductive wine, that can hopefully be ‘cured’ with aeration, versus an oxidized or TCA-infected one any day . . .

Cheers.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
larry schaffer:

Jeremy,

The problem in Australia was due to very high incidents of TCA, correct? I don’t remember it being due to random oxidation, but that has always been a problem with natural cork - and will remain so.

I remember reading the original AWRI study on closures and was blown away by the variability in trans-ox on corks, including the ‘best’ ones. It does appear that the ‘best’ corks - the ones that led to the most preferred wines - did allow in some oxygen, but very little.

Those calling for a ‘perfect’ closure where no oxygen is allowed in - this will lead to reduction, maybe sooner, maybe later. Of course, I’ll take a reductive wine, that can hopefully be ‘cured’ with aeration, versus an oxidized or TCA-infected one any day . . .

Cheers.

I remember hearing / reading same kind of anecdotal information, something how Portuguese cork producers sent a big batch of poor-quality TCA-affected corks for one vintage in the late 90’s. It didn’t take many years for the majority of Oz producers to switch to screwcaps.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
After tasting thousands of bottles sealed with screwcap from the early 2000’s, here’s the number of oxidised, TCA affected or wines that I thought were too reductive under screwcap: 0.

Even looking at the producers who are ‘so called’ immune to premox there are failures due to the cork. I reckon I’ve drink around 250 bottles of Coche over the past 20 years. Very few have been oxidised but I have had an oxidised '88 CC and a couple of late 90’s Rougeots. I have also probably had 10 bottles affected by TCA. That’s a failure rate of around 5%. Totally unacceptable. Similar story with Raveneau. Very low oxidation rate but I have had a lot of corked bottles from the 2000 vintage. Probably a failure rate in total of 7+%. Totally unacceptable.

I asked Gregory Patriat of Jean-Claude Boisset, to pull out an older white Burg he had bottled under screwcap, two weeks ago. We took a humble 2007 Jean-Claude Boisset Savigny-Les-Beaune Blanc along to lunch at La Cabotte. It was splendid, showing just the right amount of toasty development. The fruit was fresh and vibrant and the acidity of the vintage shone. A humble wine, drinking beautifully at 10 years of age.

The arguments against screwcap are white noise. Do you want 0% failure, or are you happy to always accept a degree of failure?

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

After tasting thousands of bottles sealed with screwcap from the early 2000’s, here’s the number of oxidised, TCA affected or wines that I thought were too reductive under screwcap: 0.

A propos, I’ve had one TCA-affected wine under a screwcap.

But that last claim sounds weird. I have tasted thousands of bottles sealed with screwcap and I’ve had numerous wines I’ve thought that were too reductive. Of course I’ve had many too reductive wines under a natural cork as well, so that doesn’t mean that excessive reduction would be solely because of the screwcaps. However, claiming that you’ve never had a too reductive wine bottled under a screwcap most likely means that you are either more tolerant of reduction or then you’re just forgetting some heavily reductive wines. It sounds pretty impossible that you’ve managed to avoid all the reductive wines under a screwcap out there.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Jon Bonne wrote an article about a TCA-contaminated apple he got in a salad.
This stuff is everywhere. Ditto TBA. That said,I have not had a TCA contaminated Diam cork.

I ve talked to lots of winemakers here about premox. They all agree you can change the ageability of white wines with more or less SO2. They don’t agree about glutathione. They don’t think corks are the problem but that perhaps they can be the solution. Of copurse, the old theory about wine slowly oxidizing through the cork goes out the window.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked: I have had great luck with Diam, and have asked many of our producers to switch to Diam. Then I had a batch of wine that was corked under Diam, and started worrying about it. Fortunately it turned out that the winery had an environmental TCA problem, and the whole tank was bad. Phew.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox was kind enough to send me a bottle of 2009 Ici La Bas Chardonnay “Victoria,” made by Jim Clendenen of Au Bon Climat from Anderson Valley fruit. I guess his point was to show that some California chardonnays can go the distance.

I served it blind to some wine buddies (Gray Newman included), who all liked it. Very fresh, with lively acid but also a lot of body – so much that several people wondered if it might be a Rhone white. The tip-off that it wasn’t a Rhone should have been the oak. There was a firm strand of French oak running from nose to finish, adding some complexity and structure. Anyway, a really nicely balanced, satisfying chardonnay. (And I see they still have the 2010 for $35 on the ABC website.)

Mel - I wonder who sold them the barrels? I’d love to know what kind they were.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Francois Freres Tight Grain, three year air dry, med plus toast.
The wine was fermented in barrel, went through ML in bbl, spent about 18 months sur lies.
From the Elke Vineyard outside of Boonville.

I got tired of calling on retailers etc so we stopped.
